explicit_text: My name is Masayo Matsumoto, born in 1893 in Nagano Prefecture as the second daughter of Matasaku and Toshi Matsumoto. I was raised in a family with a strong Shinto background and experienced many hardships, including illness and family struggles. Despite these challenges, I pursued education, became a teacher, and later embraced Christianity, which deeply transformed my life. My father named me Masayo, meaning 'to give politics,' reflecting his interest in politics and hopes for me.

explicit_text: I was born and raised in Nagano Prefecture, where my family lived and where my father served as a village chief. Nagano was also the place where I first encountered Christianity and began attending church services despite the distance. My father's involvement in local politics and community projects, like building a bridge over the Kiso River, were significant parts of our life in Nagano.

explicit_text: I was born in 1893, which was the 26th year of the Meiji era, a time of great change in Japan. This era shaped much of my family's history and the societal expectations we lived under.

explicit_text: When I became very sick during my teacher's college years, my father sold property to send me to Nagoya University Hospital for six months. This hospital stay was crucial for my recovery and allowed me to return to school as a returning student, which was rare at the time.

explicit_text: Shinto was my family's traditional religion, and my father was part of the Shinto priesthood for years. Our home had a Shinto altar where my mother made us pray daily, and strict rules governed our behavior around it. However, my father struggled with belief in Shinto gods, seeing them as human creations, which caused tension in our family. I eventually found Shinto practices too strict and sought spiritual comfort elsewhere.
